identify_pain.narrative: Three operational pains confirmed, all owned by Danny and the Canada terminal procurement/buyer team. Pain 1 (highest priority per 29 Jun): manual vendor follow-up on open/overdue POs. Danny said on 12 Jun 'No automated process currently to identify outstanding POs not received; manual queries or reports required to surface undelivered POs.' Buyers must manually track promised delivery dates and chase vendors when shipments slip, a repetitive, low-value task across 5 terminals. On 29 Jun Danny de-scoped Merlin Intake and said 'We would like to focus on a solution for the Open Pos follow up.' This is the top pain now. Pain 2: frequent PR transfer errors between Maximo and Oracle causing manual buyer review. Danny quantified on 12 Jun: 'buyers see ~20-25 PRs/day each (4 buyers → ~80-100/day)' due to incomplete field mappings during Maximo cloud migrations. Each PR fails the Oracle interface, gets routed to a buyer for manual inspection, then re-submitted. Pain 3: slow, manual spend analysis. On 12 Jun Danny requested 'AI-assisted analysis (Merlin/Ask Merlin) to speed insights and category grouping (e.g., laptops, spare parts)' vs current Power BI dashboards and manual Excel pivots that are time-consuming and lack ad-hoc query flexibility. Himanshu noted 'data quality gaps exist (missing country codes, inconsistent classification, 4% missing country data)' on 22 Jun. All three pains are efficiency/workload, not financial/cost (no quantified $ waste or savings target articulated). Pain owner: Danny and his 4-buyer Canada procurement team.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria emerged across the three calls and are operational/technical, not competitive. From the 12 Jun discovery: (1) automated PR validation to catch Maximo-to-Oracle mapping errors before they create buyer review work (confirmed pain ~80-100 PRs/day manually reviewed), (2) automated vendor follow-up on open/overdue POs to retrieve promised delivery dates without manual buyer outreach, (3) AI-assisted spend analysis (Merlin/Ask Merlin) for faster category grouping and pricing insights vs manual Excel pivots, and (4) cXML punchout support for strategic supplier catalogs. On the 22 Jun demo Danny and Himanshu asked about: data-lake vs batch-ingestion for real-time analytics (performance matters), API-only integration (no Maximo customization allowed on shared cluster), tax handling in PR line items, and quote upload/parsing for multi-line vendor quotes. Danny's 29 Jun email confirmed (5) open-PO follow-up is the top priority, and Merlin Intake is de-scoped ('we don't think the intake solution will streamline the current process... more convenient to invest time on training'). So the live criteria are: open-PO vendor follow-up automation (must-have), Ask Merlin spend analytics (want), API-only Maximo integration (constraint), real-time data-lake connection preferred over batch (nice-to-have), and pricing/timeline fit within FY26 budget window. No formal RFP or scored evaluation grid; Danny is driving this as an operational efficiency buy, not a strategic sourcing competition.

deltas.subject: Quantify the ROI and arm Danny for the head-office pitch. Make this a one-page executive summary Danny can forward to head office alongside the pricing, positioning him as the internal champion with a defensible business case, not just a feature requester. value.flag: Quantify the ROI and arm Danny for the head-office pitch. Danny owns the operational pain (80-100 manual PR reviews/day, 4 buyers spending capacity on validation instead of strategic sourcing) and is convinced Merlin Intake solves it, but he has not yet built the financial business case to take to head office. The 29 Jun proposal should include a simple ROI model: 100 PRs/day × 15 min/review (conservative) = 25 buyer-hours/day saved = ~6,250 hours/year (assuming 250 working days) = ~3 FTE-equivalents of capacity freed, worth $X in cost avoidance or redeployed to strategic sourcing generating $Y in savings. Layer in faster vendor follow-ups (manual today) and smarter spend analytics (Himanshu's ask) as secondary value. Make this a one-page executive summary Danny can forward to head office alongside the pricing, positioning him as the internal champion with a defensible business case, not just a feature requester. This also de-risks the 'we'll review internally' gate by giving Danny the ammunition he needs to win internal approval.
